description: To support the second phase of a Career/Research Transition award program that provides 1 -3 years of independent research support (R00) contingent on securing an independent research position. Award recipients will be expected to compete successfully for independent R01 support from the NIH during the R00 research transition award period.

grant_sections.definition: Narrative summary of the proposed activity.
grant_sections.generation_instructions: Write a concise, self-contained summary (typically 30 lines or less) of the proposed activity. The summary must clearly state the central hypothesis, the specific aims, and the overall methodology. Emphasize the novelty and expected contribution to the field. Maintain a professional, accessible tone, suitable for publication on NIH RePORTER if funded. Ensure strict adherence to NIH formatting requirements (R.110) and absolutely exclude any Personal Identifiable Information (PII) or Protected Health Information (PHI) (R.100, Step 4).
guidelines: Ensure the content is suitable for public release via NIH RePORTER if the application is funded (R.5).
Do not include Personal Identifiable Information (PII) or Protected Health Information (PHI) in the summary (R.100, Step 4).
Follow requirements for PDF files, fonts, margins, headers, footers, and citations (R.110).
search_queries: NIH RePORTER project summary examples
Writing NIH abstract for public release
Format requirements for NIH Project Summary/Abstract
Key elements of a successful NIH abstract
NIH R.5 public information requirements
grant_sections.title: Project Summary/Abstract

grant_sections.definition: Statement explaining the relevance of the research to public health.
grant_sections.generation_instructions: Write a brief, non-technical statement (typically 2-3 sentences) explaining how the proposed research addresses a public health problem. Clearly articulate the potential benefits to human health, disease prevention, or health promotion. This statement must be easily understood by the general public and suitable for publication on NIH RePORTER. Focus on the 'why it matters' rather than the 'how it works.'
guidelines: Explain how the research relates to public health, disease prevention, or health promotion.
Ensure the content is suitable for public release via NIH RePORTER if the application is funded (R.5).
Do not include Personal Identifiable Information (PII) or Protected Health Information (PHI) (R.100, Step 4).
search_queries: NIH public health relevance statement examples
Translational research impact statement NIH
How to write public health relevance for grant
NIH RePORTER public health relevance
grant_sections.title: Public Health Relevance Statement

grant_sections.definition: Description of the facilities, equipment, and resources available for the project.
grant_sections.generation_instructions: Detail the physical and intellectual environment where the research will be conducted. List all relevant facilities (e.g., lab space, animal housing, clinical research units), major equipment (>$5,000), and institutional resources (e.g., computational clusters, core labs). For multi-site projects, clearly delineate resources available at each performance site. The tone should be factual and emphasize the adequacy and suitability of the resources for the proposed work to successfully execute the Research Strategy.
guidelines: Describe the facilities, equipment, and resources available to perform the proposed research.
Detail the organizational setting and environment where the work will be conducted.
search_queries: NIH Facilities and Other Resources template
Institutional core facility description NIH
Listing specialized equipment in NIH grant
Organizational setting description for research
grant_sections.title: Facilities & Other Resources

grant_sections.definition: Detailed justification for all requested costs.
grant_sections.generation_instructions: Provide a detailed, year-by-year justification for all requested costs. For personnel, specify the role, name (if known), and exact person-months (or FTE) dedicated to the project, linking their effort directly to specific aims. Justify all non-personnel costs, especially equipment and high-cost supplies, demonstrating necessity for the proposed research. If using a Modular Budget (R.320), justify the modules and provide detailed personnel and consortium justifications separately. The tone must be precise, factual, and fiscally responsible.
guidelines: Provide a detailed justification for all personnel, supplies, equipment, and travel costs requested.
Adhere to the specific instructions for either the R&R Budget Form (R.300) or the PHS 398 Modular Budget Form (R.320).
search_queries: NIH R&R Budget justification examples
Justifying personnel effort in NIH grant
Modular budget justification requirements NIH R.320
Allowable and unallowable costs NIH
Detailed supply cost breakdown grant
grant_sections.title: Budget Justification
topics: Alignment of costs with specific aims

grant_sections.definition: Detailed information required for research involving human subjects or clinical trials (R.500).
grant_sections.generation_instructions: Complete this section following the detailed instructions provided in R.500. If a clinical trial is involved, provide a detailed description of the study design (e.g., randomization, blinding, endpoints), intervention, and statistical analysis plan. Describe the study population, inclusion/exclusion criteria, and the recruitment plan, ensuring compliance with NIH policies on inclusion of women and minorities. Detail the procedures for protecting human subjects, assessing risks and benefits, and establishing a Data Safety Monitoring Plan (DSMP). The tone must be rigorous, ethical, and compliant with federal regulations.
guidelines: Complete this section following the detailed instructions provided in R.500.
Provide all required information regarding human subjects research and clinical trial design.
search_queries: NIH R.500 Human Subjects and Clinical Trials instructions
Data Safety Monitoring Plan requirements NIH
Inclusion of women and minorities NIH policy
Defining clinical trial endpoints NIH guidance
Statistical analysis plan for clinical trial grant
IRB approval process NIH grant
Recruitment strategies for diverse cohorts
grant_sections.title: PHS Human Subjects and Clinical Trials Information
topics: Clinical trial phase and design methodology

grant_sections.definition: Concise statement of the goals and objectives of the proposed research.
grant_sections.generation_instructions: Write a concise, one-page document. Begin with a brief introductory paragraph establishing the problem, the current knowledge gap, and the central hypothesis. Follow this with 2 to 4 distinct, measurable Specific Aims. Each aim should clearly state what will be accomplished and how it will test a component of the central hypothesis. Ensure the aims are highly focused and achievable within the proposed timeline and budget. The tone must be direct, ambitious yet achievable, and highly focused.
guidelines: Clearly state the goals of the project and the specific objectives the research intends to accomplish.
Ensure the aims are concise and measurable.
search_queries: Examples of strong NIH Specific Aims page
How to structure NIH Specific Aims
Writing measurable objectives for grant proposal
Central hypothesis formulation for NIH R01
One-page specific aims template
grant_sections.title: Specific Aims

grant_sections.definition: The core scientific narrative detailing Significance, Innovation, and Approach.
grant_sections.generation_instructions: Develop a compelling narrative organized strictly under the headings: Significance, Innovation, and Approach. Significance must establish the importance of the problem and the potential impact. Innovation must describe how the project challenges current paradigms or uses novel methods/tools. Approach must detail the experimental design, methods, data analysis, and expected results for each Specific Aim, supported by preliminary data. Include a dedicated section on potential pitfalls and alternative strategies, and explicitly address plans for ensuring scientific rigor and reproducibility. The tone must be authoritative, evidence-based, and persuasive, adhering strictly to page limits (R.110).
guidelines: The narrative must be organized into three distinct parts: Significance, Innovation, and Approach.
Provide sufficient detail for reviewers to evaluate the scientific merit of the application (R.100, Step 6).
Follow requirements for PDF files, fonts, margins, headers, footers, and citations (R.110).
search_queries: NIH Research Strategy Significance section best practices
Writing NIH Innovation section examples
Detailed experimental approach NIH R01
Rigor and reproducibility plan NIH guidance
Preliminary data presentation in NIH grant
Statistical analysis plan for grant proposal
Addressing potential pitfalls and alternative strategies
NIH R.400 Research Strategy requirements
grant_sections.title: Research Strategy (Significance, Innovation, Approach)

grant_sections.definition: Detailed plan addressing the use of vertebrate animals.
grant_sections.generation_instructions: If vertebrate animals are used, provide a detailed description of the procedures, including the species, strain, age, sex, and number of animals. Justify the appropriateness of the species selected and the necessity of using animals. Describe the procedures for minimizing pain, distress, and discomfort, including the use of analgesia and anesthesia. Detail the method of euthanasia. Ensure the plan aligns with institutional IACUC policies and NIH guidelines, demonstrating commitment to animal welfare.
guidelines: If applicable, describe the procedures for minimizing pain, distress, and discomfort in animals.
Justify the use of vertebrate animals and the appropriateness of the species selected.
search_queries: NIH Vertebrate Animals Section requirements
Justification for animal model selection NIH
Minimizing pain and distress in research animals
IACUC protocol description for NIH grant
grant_sections.title: Vertebrate Animals

grant_sections.definition: Detailed plan addressing the use of select agents.
grant_sections.generation_instructions: If the research involves select agents (as defined by CDC/APHIS), clearly identify the agents and the proposed experiments. Describe the facilities where the work will be conducted, including the required biosafety level (e.g., BSL-3) and institutional registration status. Detail the procedures for containment, security, and personnel training. The tone must be highly technical, focused on regulatory compliance and risk mitigation, ensuring all federal guidelines are met.
guidelines: If applicable, describe the select agents to be used in the proposed research.
Detail the facilities, procedures, and safety measures for containment.
search_queries: NIH Select Agent Research requirements
CDC/APHIS select agent list
Biosafety level requirements for select agents
Security plan for select agent research
grant_sections.title: Select Agent Research

grant_sections.definition: Description of collaborative arrangements and subaward roles.
grant_sections.generation_instructions: For each consortium site or subaward, clearly define the specific scientific tasks they will perform (Statement of Work). Identify the key personnel at the collaborating institution and their respective roles and effort (person-months). Describe the administrative and fiscal arrangements, including how funds will be managed and how scientific progress will be coordinated and monitored between the lead institution and the consortium partner(s). Demonstrate that the arrangement is necessary and well-managed.
guidelines: Describe the roles and responsibilities of all collaborating institutions and personnel.
Detail the administrative, fiscal, and scientific arrangements for the consortium.
search_queries: NIH Consortium Arrangements description requirements
Writing Statement of Work for subaward
Administrative and fiscal arrangements for NIH consortium
Roles and responsibilities in multi-site grant
grant_sections.title: Consortium/Contractual Arrangements
topics: Roles of key personnel at collaborating sites

grant_sections.definition: Plan outlining how research resources will be shared.
grant_sections.generation_instructions: Detail the plan for sharing research resources generated by the project, such as unique reagents, cell lines, animal models, and specialized software. Specify the mechanism (e.g., public repository, MTA) and timeline for making these resources available to the scientific community. Ensure the plan explicitly addresses compliance with relevant NIH policies (e.g., Model Organism Sharing Policy, Genomic Data Sharing Policy, if applicable) and addresses any potential intellectual property issues.
guidelines: Outline the plan for sharing research resources (e.g., model organisms, reagents) generated by the project.
Ensure the plan complies with NIH policies regarding resource dissemination.
search_queries: NIH Model Organism Sharing Policy
Writing NIH Resource Sharing Plan
Material Transfer Agreement (MTA) requirements NIH
Sharing unique research reagents NIH
grant_sections.title: Resource Sharing Plan(s)
topics: Types of resources to be shared

grant_sections.definition: Plan detailing the management and sharing of scientific data.
grant_sections.generation_instructions: Provide a concise yet comprehensive plan (typically two pages) addressing the six required elements of the NIH DMS Policy. Specify the types of scientific data to be generated, the standards (metadata) to be applied, the repository chosen for preservation (e.g., NCBI, institutional), the timeline for data sharing, and the methods for oversight and compliance. Explicitly state how data sharing costs are reflected in the budget justification. The tone must be technical, policy-compliant, and demonstrate a robust strategy for maximizing data utility.
guidelines: Detail how scientific data will be managed, preserved, and accessed.
Specify the timelines and methods for sharing data with the broader research community.
search_queries: NIH Data Management and Sharing Policy 2023 requirements
Selecting NIH-approved data repository
Budgeting for NIH DMS Plan costs
Metadata standards for scientific data sharing
Writing a compliant NIH DMS Plan
Six required elements of NIH DMS Plan
grant_sections.title: Data Management and Sharing (DMS) Plan
topics: Roles and responsibilities for DMS oversight
Budget justification for data sharing costs

grant_sections.definition: Placeholder for any additional required plans specified by the FOA.
grant_sections.generation_instructions: Use this section to address any required plans not covered elsewhere, such as the mandatory Authentication of Key Biological and/or Chemical Resources. If required, detail the methods used to verify the identity and validity of key resources (e.g., cell lines, antibodies, chemical compounds) used in the research, ensuring rigor and reproducibility. Also, address compliance with the Sex as a Biological Variable (SABV) policy if not covered in the Research Strategy. Ensure all specific mandates from the Funding Opportunity Announcement (FOA) are addressed here clearly and concisely.
guidelines: Address any additional required plans mandated by the specific Funding Opportunity Announcement (FOA).
Ensure compliance with any specific NIH policy requirements not covered in standard sections (e.g., Authentication of Key Biological and/or Chemical Resources).
search_queries: NIH Authentication of Key Biological and/or Chemical Resources plan
Sex as a Biological Variable (SABV) policy NIH
Required research plans NIH FOA specific
Guidelines for authenticating cell lines and antibodies
grant_sections.title: Other Plan(s)

name: Research Transition Award
